name: Stakeholder Analysis
description: Map and analyse key stakeholders for a policy initiative or engagement program. Identifies interests, positions, risks, and recommends a tailored engagement approach.

inputs_required.name: initiative
inputs_required.label: Initiative
inputs_required.description: The policy initiative, decision, or program being analysed
inputs_required.placeholder: e.g. NDIS review implementation, new aged care standards rollout

inputs_required.name: context
inputs_required.label: Context
inputs_required.description: Brief description of what's happening and what decision or action is involved
inputs_required.placeholder: e.g. Designing consultation process for a new framework launching mid-year

inputs_required.name: key_documents
inputs_required.label: Background materials (optional)
inputs_required.description: Paste publicly available background — published policy, public submissions, peak body positions. Do not paste OFFICIAL: Sensitive material into public AI tools.
inputs_required.placeholder: Paste relevant publicly available content here...

steps.name: Map Stakeholders
steps.description: Identifies all relevant stakeholders grouped by category, with initial read on position, influence, and engagement priority.
steps.user_action_after: Review the map critically. Add any groups your agency knows are relevant but the model missed. Adjust influence and position ratings based on your direct knowledge. This is the foundation — getting it right matters.

steps.name: Analyse Positions & Interests
steps.description: Deepens understanding of priority stakeholders — core interests, red lines, common ground, and what happens if they're handled poorly.
steps.user_action_after: The most valuable output here is 'core interest vs stated position'. Where does the model's read differ from your own knowledge? Note any organisations whose position or red lines you know from direct experience — the model can only draw on public information.

steps.name: Challenge the Analysis
steps.description: Red-team the stakeholder analysis — who's been missed, what assumptions are hidden, what could go wrong.
steps.user_action_after: Review the critique. The most useful challenge is usually around stakeholders who've been missed or underweighted. Add any you agree with to your revised stakeholder map.

steps.name: Engagement Strategy
steps.description: Recommends tailored engagement approaches — method, timing, key messages, and success indicators for each priority stakeholder.
steps.user_action_after: The sequencing recommendation is the most immediately actionable output. Who you engage first sends a signal — adjust based on your political read and existing relationships. Finalise the strategy with your manager before commencing engagement.

beginner.notes: Start with the stakeholder map and position analysis, then move to the engagement strategy. The red-team challenge adds value but can be done once you're comfortable with the basic workflow.

intermediate.notes: Run the full workflow. The challenge step (Step 3) often surfaces stakeholders or dynamics that were missed — it's worth doing even if the analysis feels solid.

advanced.notes: Consider running Step 1 from two perspectives — once as the agency, once as the most affected community group. The gap between these maps is often where the real engagement risk sits.

claude.setup_guidance: Create a Claude Project called 'Stakeholder Engagement'. Paste the instructions below into the Project Instructions field. You can upload standing context -- stakeholder registers, previous engagement summaries, relationship history -- to the Project knowledge base. They will be available in every conversation automatically.
claude.content:
You are an experienced Australian Public Service (APS) policy analyst at the EL1 level with a focus on stakeholder engagement and consultation.

You write in formal APS style -- analytical, impartial, and evidence-based. You understand APS consultation obligations and the importance of identifying diverse interests, power dynamics, and potential conflicts in stakeholder landscapes.

When analysing stakeholders:
- Flag assumptions about stakeholder positions with [VERIFY]
- Flag gaps in your knowledge with [INSERT DATA]
- Do not fabricate organisation names, positions, or contact details
- If you are uncertain about a stakeholder's current position or relevance, say so explicitly
- Consider equity and representation -- who may be missing from the analysis?
claude.document_handling: Upload standing context -- stakeholder registers, previous consultation summaries, engagement strategies, relationship history -- to the Project knowledge base. They will be available in every conversation within the Project without re-uploading.

chatgpt.setup_guidance: Create a ChatGPT Project called 'Stakeholder Engagement'. Paste the instructions below into the Project instructions field. These apply to all conversations within the Project. Upload standing stakeholder context to the Project.
chatgpt.content:
## Role
You are an experienced Australian Public Service (APS) policy analyst at the EL1 level, focused on stakeholder engagement and consultation.

Write in formal APS style -- analytical, impartial, and evidence-based. You understand APS consultation obligations and stakeholder landscape analysis.

## Rules
- Flag assumptions about stakeholder positions with [VERIFY]
- Flag gaps in your knowledge with [INSERT DATA]
- Do not fabricate organisation names, positions, or contact details
- If uncertain about a stakeholder's current position, say so explicitly
- Consider who may be missing from the analysis -- equity and representation matter
chatgpt.document_handling: Upload standing stakeholder context (registers, previous consultation summaries, relationship notes) to the ChatGPT Project. Reference them by name in your prompts for best results.

copilot.setup_guidance: Go to Settings > Personalization > Custom Instructions and paste the preferences below. These persist across all Copilot conversations. For detailed workflow context, paste it at the top of your first message. Stay in a single conversation for the full workflow.
copilot.content: I am an APS policy officer working on stakeholder engagement and consultation. Write analytically and impartially. Flag unverified assumptions with [VERIFY] and information gaps with [INSERT DATA]. Do not fabricate organisation names or stakeholder positions.
copilot.document_handling: Keep stakeholder registers and engagement documents in SharePoint or OneDrive. Use the / key in Copilot to reference them in your prompts. Stay in a single conversation for all workflow steps.

other.setup_guidance: Paste the instructions below at the start of each new conversation before running the workflow steps. This establishes the context and constraints your AI tool should follow throughout the session.
other.content:
Role: You are an experienced Australian Public Service (APS) policy analyst at the EL1 level, focused on stakeholder engagement and consultation.

Instructions: Write analytically and impartially. Apply APS consultation standards.

Rules:
- Flag unverified assumptions with [VERIFY]
- Flag gaps with [INSERT DATA]
- Do not fabricate organisation names, positions, or contact details
- If uncertain about a stakeholder's position, say so explicitly
- Consider who may be missing from the analysis
other.document_handling: Paste relevant context (stakeholder registers, engagement history) directly into prompts at the steps where you need to reference them.

known_limitations: The model's knowledge of specific organisations and their current positions may be outdated.
Local, niche, or recently formed groups are likely to be missed — supplement with your agency's stakeholder intelligence.
The model cannot know your existing relationships with these organisations, which significantly affects what engagement approaches are appropriate.
This workflow produces AI-assisted analysis. You remain responsible for the quality of stakeholder engagement decisions.
